content: 🚀 Raspberry Pi Remote Access via Twingate + WireGuard Use your Raspberry Pi as a secure home VPN exit node with zero port forwarding
title: Raspberry Pi + Twingate + WireGuard

content: 0. Prerequisites Raspberry Pi (OS: Bullseye or Bookworm, 64-bit) SSH or physical access to the Pi A Twingate tenant (free plan is fine) Your Pi has a static IP or DHCP reservation Get Pi’s IP: hostname -I Update the Pi: sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade -y
title: Raspberry Pi + Twingate + WireGuard

content: 1. Create Remote Network & Connector in Twingate Log into the Twingate Admin Console. Create a Remote Network (e.g., Home-Network). Under Connectors, click an unused Connector → Deploy Connector. Select Linux (systemd). Keep this window open — you’ll copy the script into the Pi.
title: Raspberry Pi + Twingate + WireGuard

content: 2. Install Twingate Connector on Raspberry Pi Run the script provided by the Twingate console on your Raspberry Pi. It looks like: curl -fsSL https://b.twingate.com/download/linux/setup.sh \ | sudo TWINGATE_URL="YOUR_TENANT.twingate.com" \ TWINGATE_ACCESS_TOKEN="YOUR_ACCESS_TOKEN" \ TWINGATE_REFRESH_TOKEN="YOUR_REFRESH_TOKEN" \ bash Once installed, the Connector should show Connected in the Twingate console.
title: Raspberry Pi + Twingate + WireGuard

content: 3. Install WireGuard on Raspberry Pi
title: Raspberry Pi + Twingate + WireGuard

content: 3.1 Install packages sudo apt update sudo apt install -y wireguard wireguard-tools
title: Raspberry Pi + Twingate + WireGuard

content: 3.2 Generate server keys sudo -i umask 077 wg genkey | tee /etc/wireguard/server_privatekey | wg pubkey > /etc/wireguard/server_publickey SERVER_PRIV_KEY=$(cat /etc/wireguard/server_privatekey) SERVER_PUB_KEY=$(cat /etc/wireguard/server_publickey)
title: Raspberry Pi + Twingate + WireGuard

content: 3.3 Create WireGuard server configuration Create /etc/wireguard/wg0.conf: cat > /etc/wireguard/wg0.conf <<EOF [Interface] Address = 10.8.0.1/24 ListenPort = 51820 PrivateKey = $SERVER_PRIV_KEY SaveConfig = true ## NAT for VPN clients PostUp = iptables -t nat -A POSTROUTING -o eth0 -j MASQUERADE; \ iptables -A FORWARD -i eth0 -o wg0 -j ACCEPT; \ iptables -A FORWARD -i wg0 -o eth0 -j ACCEPT PostDown = iptables -t nat -D POSTROUTING -o eth0 -j MASQUERADE; \ iptables -D FORWARD -i eth0 -o wg0 -j ACCEPT; \ iptables -D FORWARD -i wg0 -o eth0 -j ACCEPT EOF If using Wi-Fi, replace eth0 with wlan0.
title: Raspberry Pi + Twingate + WireGuard

content: 3.4 Enable IP forwarding echo "net.ipv4.ip_forward=1" | sudo tee -a /etc/sysctl.conf sudo sysctl -p
title: Raspberry Pi + Twingate + WireGuard

content: 3.5 Start WireGuard sudo systemctl enable wg-quick@wg0 sudo systemctl start wg-quick@wg0 sudo systemctl status wg-quick@wg0
title: Raspberry Pi + Twingate + WireGuard

content: 4. Expose ONLY WireGuard (UDP 51820) via Twingate In Twingate Admin Console: Go to Resources → Add Resource Name: wireguard-home Address: Pi LAN IP, e.g., 192.168.1.50 Enable Port Restrictions Add: UDP 51820 Assign to a Group (e.g., Admins) Ensure your user is in that Group This prevents any other Pi ports from being remotely reachable.
title: Raspberry Pi + Twingate + WireGuard

content: 5. Create a WireGuard Client (Peer)
title: Raspberry Pi + Twingate + WireGuard

content: 5.1 Generate client keys umask 077 wg genkey | tee ~/laptop_privatekey | wg pubkey > ~/laptop_publickey LAPTOP_PRIV_KEY=$(cat ~/laptop_privatekey) LAPTOP_PUB_KEY=$(cat ~/laptop_publickey)
title: Raspberry Pi + Twingate + WireGuard

content: 5.2 Add the peer to the server config Edit /etc/wireguard/wg0.conf: [Peer] PublicKey = LAPTOP_PUBLIC_KEY_HERE AllowedIPs = 10.8.0.2/32 Restart WireGuard: sudo systemctl restart wg-quick@wg0
title: Raspberry Pi + Twingate + WireGuard

content: 6. Create Client WireGuard Config Create a file called laptop.conf: [Interface] PrivateKey = LAPTOP_PRIVATE_KEY_HERE Address = 10.8.0.2/32 DNS = 1.1.1.1 [Peer] PublicKey = SERVER_PUBLIC_KEY_HERE AllowedIPs = 0.0.0.0/0, ::/0 Endpoint = wireguard-home:51820 PersistentKeepalive = 25 Important points: The Endpoint uses the Twingate Resource name, not a public address. All traffic (0.0.0.0/0) will exit through your home internet.
title: Raspberry Pi + Twingate + WireGuard

content: 7. Connect From Remote Device On your laptop/phone: Install Twingate Client Log in to your tenant Turn Twingate ON Install WireGuard app Import laptop.conf Turn WireGuard tunnel ON Your traffic now flows: Device → Twingate → Raspberry Pi (WireGuard) → Home Internet No port forwarding. No public exposure. Fully encrypted end-to-end.
title: Raspberry Pi + Twingate + WireGuard

content: 8. Verification Check public IP (should match your home network): curl ifconfig.me Or open https://ifconfig.me in a browser.
title: Raspberry Pi + Twingate + WireGuard

content: Simply, Tmux is a free and open-source terminal multiplexer for Unix-based systems. It does support multiple windows, panes and detachable sessions. It allows efficient and smart way to manage independent sessions, all in the same viewable terminal. Tmux is really important and life-saver for those who spend much of their working hours connecting to various servers and doing command line tasks, e.g. administrators, developers, integrators, and operation engineers. Tmux have hundreds of keyboard shortcuts that speeds up things and enable efficient and smart ways of working. In this tutorial, I will explain how to set up Tmux, and Tmuxinator, which is basically a ruby gem that builds on top of tmux to simplify managing complex tmux sessions. With Tmuxinator you can build your project (set of tmux windows/sessions/panes) using yaml configuration files which can be used as templates, easily shared, and upgradable.
title: Using Tmux instead of paid SSH Clients

content: Setup
title: Using Tmux instead of paid SSH Clients

content: 1. Set up WSL Here you can set up WSL on your workstation. Make sure that you set WSL version to 2. Install Ubuntu on WSL2 Use this link to install Ubuntu on your windows workstation.
title: Using Tmux instead of paid SSH Clients

content: Install Tmux Update the setup Ubuntu distro. sudo apt update sudo apt upgrade -y Setup Tmux sudo apt install tmux Now you can launch Tmux, using tmux command. Set up Tmuxinator sudo apt-get install -y tmuxinator
title: Using Tmux instead of paid SSH Clients

content: Sample Setup Below are steps to set up your own tmuxinator project Assuming you always access server X, and server Y. On each, once you login, you would check filesystem size, pods status of a cluster, and tail a log file For the purpose of the completeness of this example, there will be steps involved to configure tmux itself to meet our intentions. Some important files/directories involved: 1- .tmux.conf This is tmux configuration file. Usually you create this on your home directory or some .config directory under it. 2- project.yaml : This will be the file that we will be generated (and we’ll modify it) once you create a new tmuxinator project 3- ~/.config/tmuxinator This is the default directory where new tmuxinator project files, i.e. yamls are stored Modifications in .tmux.conf These lines, I modified/added, are totally optional, but I thought to include set-option -g mouse on : This will enable mouse usage in tmux; very helpful Default Tmux key-prefix is Ctrl-b, which, to me, felt a bit too much, so I wanted to change it to Ctrl-Space, below 3 lines does the thing unbind C-b set-option -g prefix C-Space bind-key C-Space send-prefix set-option -g window-active-style 'fg=lightgreen' Set the font of the active window/pane to lightgreen Below 3 lines customize pane borders. For each pane, it sets the fore- and back-ground colors, plus pane title written in the border set -g pane-border-status bottom set -g pane-border-format "#{pane_index} #{pane_title}" set-option -g pane-border-format "#{?#{==:#{pane_title},Main},#[fg=white bg=red],#{?#{==:#{pane_title},Disk_Space},#[fg=black bg=yellow],#{?#{==:#{pane_title},Pods},#[fg=white bg=green],#{?#{==:#{pane_title},tail},#[fg=white bg=blue],#{?#{==:#{pane_title},WhoamI},#[fg=white bg=brown],#[default]}}}}}#{pane_index} #{pane_title}" Now is time to initialize our sample project tmuxinator new First First.yaml will be create in ~/.config/tmuxinator/ The main section is the last one, called windows Before it, there are many parameters that can be configured and could be very useful, say to automatically enable logging of sessions at start up. You can google these parameters should you want to customize more. First.yaml: windows: - Server X: layout: tiled panes: - Main: - echo 'Main X pane' - Filesystem: - df -kh - Pods: - kubectl get pods -A - Tail: - tail -10f /var/log/dpkg.log - Server Y: layout: tiled panes: - Main: - echo 'Main Y pane' - Filesystem: - df -kh - Pods: - kubectl get pods -A - Tail: - tail -10f /var/log/dpkg.log To run it: tmuxinator First By default, pane titles will default to hostname (redacted), and this can be changed via below command, for each pane you select do: Ctrl+Space :select-pane -T <title> And it will change to:
title: Using Tmux instead of paid SSH Clients
